title: Phlegmariurus villonacensis
intro: Phlegmariurus villonacensis, also known as Villonac's climbing fern or Villonac's resurrection fern, is a species of epiphytic, evergreen fern native to the tropical forests of Central and South America. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Phlegmariurus villonacensis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In traditional medicine, Phlegmariurus villonacensis is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is also used in the production of herbal teas and infusions.
taxonomy: Phlegmariurus villonacensis belongs to the plant family Lycopodiaceae, commonly known as the club moss family. This classification places it within the division Lycopodiophyta, which includes other vascular plants that produce cones and have a distinct life cycle. The genus Phlegmariurus is a group of evergreen, terrestrial epiphytes that are native to tropical and subtropical regions. The classification of Phlegmariurus villonacensis further places it in the order Lycopodiales, which is a group of vascular plants that have a distinctive leafless stem and microphyllous leaves. At the kingdom level, Phlegmariurus villonacensis is classified as Plantae, indicating its status as a flowering plant.

Its extracts have been found to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which may contribute to its potential health benefits. The plant is believed to possess antimicrobial and antifungal properties, making it useful in treating infections and wounds.
